papers.title: S-stars in the Galactic center and hypervelocity stars in the Galactic halo: two faces of the tidal breakup of stellar binaries by the central massive black hole?
papers.author_names: Fupeng Zhang Youjun Lu Qingjuan Yu

abstract.text: In this paper, we investigate the link between the hypervelocity stars (HVSs) discovered in the Galactic halo and the S-stars moving in the Galactic center (GC), under the hypothesis that they are both the products of the tidal breakup of the same population of stellar binaries by the central massive black hole (MBH). By adopting several hypothetical models for binaries to be injected into the vicinity of the MBH and doing numerical simulations, we realize the tidal breakup processes of the binaries and their follow-up evolution. We find that many statistical properties of the detected HVSs and S-stars can be reproduced under some binary injecting models, and their number ratio can be reproduced if the stellar initial mass function is top-heavy (e.g., with slope ~-1.6). The total number of the captured companions is ~50 that have masses in the range ~3-7Msun and semimajor axes <~4000 AU and survive to the present within their main-sequence lifetime. The innermost one is expected to have a semimajor axis ~300-1500 AU and a pericenter distance ~10-200 AU, with a significant probability of being closer to the MBH than S2. Future detection of such a closer star would offer an important test to general relativity. The majority of the surviving ejected companions of the S-stars are expected to be located at Galactocentric distances <~20 kpc, and have heliocentric radial velocities ~-500-1500 km/s and proper motions up to ~5-20 mas/yr. Future detection of these HVSs may provide evidence for the tidal-breakup formation mechanism of the S-stars.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper proposes a unified tidal breakup mechanism where stellar binaries near the Galactic Center's massive black hole (MBH) are disrupted, producing both Galactic Center S-stars (captured companions) and hypervelocity stars (ejected components). Numerical simulations show that a top-heavy initial mass function (slope ∼−1.6) reproduces observed number ratios between HVSs and S-stars, with ∼50 companions surviving with masses 3–7 M⊙ and semimajor axes up to ∼4000 AU; the innermost companion is expected to orbit within 300–1500 AU (closer than S2), offering a test of general relativity. Most ejected companions are predicted to reside at galactocentric distances <20 kpc with radial velocities −500 to 1500 km/s and proper motions up to 5–20 mas/yr, providing a detectable population for future surveys.

source_fields.title: S-stars in the Galactic center and hypervelocity stars in the Galactic halo: two faces of the tidal breakup of stellar binaries by the central massive black hole?

source_fields.sections_text: 7. Conclusions: This section concludes that GC S-stars and hypervelocity stars (HVSs) are likely products of tidal breakup of stellar binaries near the Galactic center, with their observed properties—such as semimajor axes, velocities, and eccentricities—being naturally linked through binary breakup dynamics, and that specific binary injection rates and initial mass functions are required to reproduce both populations. 1. Introduction: This paper makes the novel contribution of simultaneously investigating the properties and kinematic correlations between hypervelocity stars in the Galactic halo and their captured companions near the Galactic center, providing a unified framework to resolve the paradox of youth by linking their origins to tidal breakup of stellar binaries in the central disk. 3. Monte Carlo Simulations: This section describes the Monte Carlo simulations of tidal breakup of stellar binaries by the central massive black hole, using detailed initial conditions and numerical methods to generate and analyze hypervelocity stars and GC S-stars. 6. The innermost captured star: The innermost captured low-mass stars ($3$–$7M_{\\odot}$) have a high probability (61%–99%) of orbiting within the S2 orbit, with pericenter distances as close as 50 AU, offering a promising opportunity for future observations to test general relativity near the supermassive black hole. 4. Orbital evolution of the captured stars: The orbital evolution of captured stars in the galactic center is primarily driven by resonant relaxation, which efficiently isotropizes their distributions and evolves their eccentricities within a timescale compatible with observational constraints, outperforming two-body relaxation. 5. Ejected HVSs in the Galactic bulge and halo: This section describes how stellar binaries disrupted near the galactic center produce hypervelocity stars (HVSs) that either escape to the halo or return to the galactic center, with their trajectories and detectability depending on the Galactic potential model and binary properties. 2. Overview: tidal breakup of stellar binaries in the vicinity of an MBH: Tidal breakup of stellar binaries near a massive black hole can produce hypervelocity stars with velocities depending on binary properties, orbital orientation, and penetration depth, with a Gaussian scatter around the mean velocity predicted by the given formula.

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical/numerical simulation paper investigating formation mechanisms. No observational data tables, candidate lists, or analysis of actual stellar objects presented. Predicts properties of future detectable objects but does not compile or analyze existing observational catalogs.

papers.title: The growth of massive stars via stellar collisions in ensemble star clusters
papers.author_names: M. S. Fujii S. Portegies Zwart

abstract.text: Recent simulations and observations suggest that star clusters form via the assembling of smaller sub-clusters. Because of their short relaxation time, sub-clusters experience core collapse much earlier than virialized solo-clusters, which have similar properties of the merger remnant of the assembling clusters. As a consequence it seems that the assembling clusters result in efficient multiple collisions of stars in the cluster core. We performed a series of $N$-body simulations of ensemble and solitary clusters including stellar collisions and found that the efficiency of multiple collisions between stars are suppressed if sub-clusters assemble after they experience core collapse individually. In this case, sub-clusters form their own multiple collision stars which experienced a few collisions, but they fail to collide with each other after their host sub-clusters assemble. The multiple collision stars scatter each other and escape, and furthermore the central density of the remnant clusters had already been depleted for the stars to experience more collisions. On the other hand, if sub-clusters assemble before they experience core collapse, the multiple collisions of stars proceed efficiently in the remnant cluster, and the collision products are more massive than virialized solo-clusters and comparable in mass to cold solo-clusters.

triage.reason: Weak title match: stellar interactions in dense systems LLM confirmed: Paper discusses stellar collisions leading to ejection/escape of collision products, which can produce runaway/high-velocity stars.

source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Introduction: This paper introduces a novel mechanism by which kinematically cold, young massive star clusters can achieve core collapse before the main-sequence lifetime of their most massive stars, enabling high stellar collision rates that overcome mass loss and facilitate the formation of intermediate-mass black holes. Solo-cluster models: The paper proposes a simulation-based method to study stellar collision runaways in virialized solo clusters, where core collapse triggers repeated stellar collisions that lead to the growth of massive stars, with primary collision products (PCPs) reaching up to ~400 M☉ before explosion, while secondary collision products (SCPs) remain less massive and either merge with PCPs or lose mass through evolution. Summary and Discussion: Ensemble star clusters evolve via two pathways—early assembling (where sub-clusters merge before core collapse, leading to dynamically mature systems and PCPs up to ~1000M⊙) or late assembling (where core collapse precedes merging, limiting PCP growth to ~100–400M⊙ and producing multiple scattered massive stars), with the maximum PCP mass depending on assembling time and stellar collision efficiency, potentially explaining IMBH formation or observed high-velocity massive stars like VFTS 682. Ensemble-cluster models: The paper proposes an ensemble-cluster model where sub-clusters merge to form a single cluster, with the core idea that the timing of assembly relative to sub-cluster core collapse determines whether massive stellar binaries (PCPs) form efficiently—early assembly leads to rapid mass segregation and PCP growth, while late assembly results in scattered, non-growing PCPs due to binary interactions. Method and Initial Conditions: The paper proposes an $N$-body simulation method to study stellar collision-driven massive star growth in solo and ensemble star clusters, using King models with varying concentrations and initial configurations (spherical or filamentary) to explore how cluster structure and dynamics influence star formation and evolution.

title: The growth of massive stars via stellar collisions in ensemble star clusters

catalog_assessment.evidence: Pure N-body simulation study of stellar collisions in cluster formation scenarios. No observational data, source IDs, or actual stellar measurements presented. Focus is entirely on numerical modeling of collision processes.
